title: Gulf allies rush to rebuild air defenses after Iran strikes expose gaps
content:
[Dubai Airshow Opens Highlighting Aerospace Industries Newest Tech]

After weeks of intense air attacks depleted regional defenses, U.S. allies in the Middle East are racing to replenish missile systems and counter new threats, particularly cheap drones, The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday.

Countries including Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates are expanding beyond traditional U.S. suppliers, exploring South Korean missile systems, Ukrainian interceptor drones and even rapid-fire guns as quicker, lower-cost solutions.

The urgency reflects how unprepared the region was for the scale of Iran’s retaliatory strikes, as well as the growing effectiveness of low-cost drone warfare. At the same time, global arms production, especially in the United States, is struggling to keep pace with demand, leaving some orders delayed by years.

Gulf states are also pursuing defense partnerships with Ukraine and engaging newer suppliers, including startups offering cheaper anti-drone technologies. But even these sources face supply constraints, as Ukraine prioritizes its own war needs.

With traditional systems like Patriot missiles in short supply, defense officials are increasingly focused on what can be delivered quickly. The scramble highlights a broader shift: in a world of prolonged conflict, speed and adaptability may matter more than relying solely on established, high-end weapons systems.

title: Ondas Trades at a Premium Valuation: How to Approach the Stock?
content:
Ondas, Inc. ONDS is trading at a forward 12-month price-to-sales ratio of 9.14X, a premium compared with the Zacks Wireless National industry’s 1.84X and the Zacks Computer and Technology sector’s 6.04X.Zacks Investment Research

Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

The company’s lofty premium valuation raises an important question for investors. Does this accurately capture the company’s long-term opportunity, or is it running ahead of fundamentals?

Let’s break it down.

ONDS’ Explosive Growth Masks Several Challenges

At first glance, ONDS appears as a compelling story. 

Ondas entered 2026 following a transformational year marked by a pivot to autonomous systems, aggressive portfolio expansion and platform scaling. 

Revenues surged 629% for the fourth quarter of 2025, while full-year revenues were up 605% to $50.7 million. Building on this momentum, management raised its 2026 revenue outlook to at least $375 million, from an earlier target of $170-$180 million.Zacks Investment Research

Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

At the heart of ONDS explosive growth is Ondas Autonomous Systems (“OAS”) division. OAS has quickly become a comprehensive “system-of-systems” platform. The division is now a multi-domain autonomy platform spanning Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance or ISR, Counter-UAS, loitering munitions/strike systems, unmanned ground vehicles and stratospheric sensing via World View acquisition.

An ambitious acquisition strategy is fueling Ondas’ expansion. In the first quarter of 2026, ONDS announced five acquisitions (World View, Mistral, Rotron, BIRD and INDO Earth). These acquisitions add strategic capabilities while expanding the addressable market for Ondas. Last year, the company acquired several companies, including Sentrycs, Apeiro Motion, Zickel and Roboteam. 

However, Ondas’ acquisition spree raises an important concern. The company is not scaling a proven business model, but it is assembling one through aggressive deal-making.

ONDS’ bullish guidance for 2026 is anchored mostly by its inorganic efforts. The company expects the five acquisitions announced in the first quarter of 2026 to add $230 million to revenues. This is a little problematic as so many acquisitions in such a short period can create integration overload and execution risks, as achieving targets depends on timely integration and conversion of backlog into revenues.Zacks Investment Research

Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

Further, profitability remains elusive. In the fourth quarter of 2025, operating expenses increased to $36.1 million, up from $9.4 million in the prior-year quarter, mainly driven by M&A activity, infrastructure investments and higher personnel costs. As a result, adjusted EBITDA loss widened to $9.9 million compared with a loss of $7 million in the year-ago quarter. Net loss was $101 million for the fourth quarter and $133.4 million for the full year. 

Adjusted EBITDA losses are expected to widen in the first quarter due to higher operating expenses, including increased leadership hiring and marketing investments to support rapid growth. Ondas expects EBITDA margins to improve over the year and expects product-level profitability by the third quarter of 2026. However, OAS profitability is expected by the third quarter of 2027, and more importantly, company-wide profitability only by the first quarter of 2028. 

While management characterizes these investments as necessary to support growth, this can pose a significant concern for investors. 

Another concern is the ballooning pro forma cash balance. The company has resorted to financing ($1.8 billion since mid-2025), and this has boosted the pro forma cash balance to $1.5 billion. ONDS highlights this as an advantage to scale rapidly. However, cash generation remains a problem. The company used $38.7 million in operating activities for 2025 and expects cash usage to increase in the first half of 2026.

Story Continues

Zacks Investment Research

Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

Heavy reliance on OAS for revenue growth in the increasingly crowded drone space is also concerning. 

The drone industry is experiencing rapid growth, with the unmanned aerial vehicle drones market expected to witness a CAGR of 16.77% from 2026 to 2035, according to a report from Precedence Research. Competition has intensified with drone companies such as Red Cat Holdings RCAT, AeroVironment AVAV and Unusual Machines UMAC vying to capture a larger share. 

For ONDS, if a single large customer delays, reduces or cancels, revenues would decline materially. Also, revenues from Ondas Networks are expected to remain modest due to uncertain rail network buildout timelines.Zacks Investment Research

Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

Given these factors, analysts have significantly revised their earnings estimates downwards for ONDS’ current year and the next year.

ONDS Stock in the Red

After a meteoric rise in 2025, ONDS is pulling back from its all-time highs. The stock has lost 30.7% in the last three months.Zacks Investment Research

Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

AVAV and UMAC has lost 52.1% and 15.8% over the same time frame while RCAT is almost at breakeven. 

The forward 12-month price/sales multiple for AVAV, UMAC and RCAT stand at 4.09X, 19.24X and 7.85X, respectively.

Conclusion: How to Approach ONDS Stock?

ONDS does offer compelling long-term potential while expanding the autonomous systems platform and strengthening its presence in the fast-growing drone market. 

However, much of this opportunity remains forward-looking. Ballooning losses and risks tied to rapid scaling and acquisitions warrant a cautious approach in the near term. Its premium valuation exposes investors to sharp volatility and heightens downside risk in the near to medium term.

Until Ondas can demonstrate sustainable organic growth and make progress toward profitability, investors need to exercise caution. 

At present, ONDS carries a Zacks Rank #5 (Strong Sell). 

You can see the complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank (Strong Buy) stocks here.

title: Ondas (ONDS) Positioned to Capitalize on Increased Military Budget
content:
Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) is one of the 7 best unmanned aerial vehicle stocks to buy now.

On March 26, Maxim increased its price target on Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) from $16 to $22 while maintaining a Buy rating on the stock. The company is in an advantageous position to benefit from an increased military budget and the requirement for independent solutions for defense with its global system of systems approach. The firm also stated that Ondas currently has enough cash to sustain its operations until 2028, when it would reach its breakeven point.

Alexey Yuzhakov/Shutterstock.com

On March 23, Ondas Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) announced $50.7 million in revenue, far more than the $7.2 million made last year. CEO Eric Brock stated that 2025 was a real turning point for the company. Things took off after the company was able to successfully combine all its different technologies into a single platform.

The greatest achievement was made possible through Ondas Autonomous Systems, which was the impetus for the company’s greatest performance to date. Brock also noted that the company started 2026 with a growing backlog and more momentum than ever before.

The company closed 2025 with over $1.5 billion in pro forma cash on hand, giving the team the power to grow operations continuously and take advantage of new opportunities. Long-term value creation remains the focus as the company plans to execute its strategy to drive sustained growth.

Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) develops and sells private wireless networks, autonomous drones, and automated data solutions and platforms through its two segments, i.e., Ondas Networks and Ondas Autonomous Systems. Its offerings include FullMAX, FullMAX SDR, Optimus, Insightful, and Raider, which have mission-critical applications.

While we acknowledge the potential of ONDS as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. title: Palantir-Connected Ondas Clinches Yet Another High-Growth Defense Pact
content:
Quick Read

Ondas Holdings (ONDS) merged with Mistral for $175M to gain prime-contractor access to over $1B in existing Defense Department IDIQ contracts, while its 4M Defense subsidiary secured a $15.8M initial order for border demining with follow-on work expected to exceed $50M across Israel’s $1.7B Eastern Border Security Barrier Initiative. AeroVironment (AVAV) faces competitive pressure from Ondas’ accelerating growth trajectory. Ondas shifted from niche technology provider to full defense prime contractor through the Mistral acquisition, gaining direct access to U.S. Army and SOCOM contracts while layering Palantir’s AI across its autonomous platforms. If you're focused on picking the right stocks and ETFs you may be missing the bigger picture: retirement income. That is exactly what The Definitive Guide to Retirement Income was created to solve, and it's free today. Read more here

With President Trump seeking $1.5 trillion for U.S. defense budgets amid rising global tensions and autonomous systems reshaping everything from border security to battlefield ops, it has the potential to reshape contractors both large and small. Ondas Holdings (NASDAQ: ONDS) is one rising player that is already seizing the opportunity.

Days after announcing a strategic partnership with Palantir Technologies (NYSE:PLTR) to layer AI across its drone and robot platforms, the company sealed a merger that hands it direct prime-contractor access to more than $1 billion in existing War Dept. contracts. Now, its building on those wins with its latest execution step: a large-scale border demining contract that is expected to exceed $50 million.

The Merger That Delivers Prime Contractor Access

Last month, Ondas announced a definitive merger agreement with Mistral, a Bethesda, Md.-based defense prime contractor with decades of experience supplying the U.S. Army, U.S. Special Operations Command, and federal agencies. The all-stock deal, valued at $175 million, includes $122.5 million in common stock paid upfront and in installments plus $52.5 million held in escrow, with closing expected in the second quarter of 2026, according to Ondas’ official press release on ir.ondas.com.

If you're focused on picking the right stocks and ETFs you may be missing the bigger picture: retirement income. That is exactly what The Definitive Guide to Retirement Income was created to solve, and it's free today. Read more here

Mistral currently serves as prime contractor on more than $1 billion of Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contracts covering unmanned aerial systems, counter-drone solutions, weapon systems integration, and sustainment services. That status gives Ondas immediate entry to U.S. Army and SOCOM contract vehicles it could not bid on directly before -- plus U.S.-based manufacturing, integration, and federal contracting infrastructure.

Story Continues

In short, Ondas moves from niche technology provider to full prime participant in multi-year DoD programs. The company’s March earnings release already reflects the momentum: it raised full-year 2026 revenue guidance to at least $375 million, a nearly sevenfold increase from 2025’s $50.7 million and more than double the prior outlook that excluded 2026 acquisitions.

Demining Order Shows the Platform in Real-World Action

A day after the Mistral news, Ondas’ subsidiary 4M Defense received a $15.8 million initial operational order under a competitive tender won in February. That base program targeted legacy minefields covering roughly 741 acres along the Israel-Syria border.

However, this morning,  Ondas clarified and expanded the picture. Near-term and follow-on orders for the demining operations are expected to exceed $50 million in program value. Work on the $15.8 million initial order launches in the coming weeks. The project forms the early phases of Israel’s $1.7 billion Eastern Border Security Barrier Initiative -- a 500-kilometer national effort led by the Ministry of Defense to upgrade the entire eastern frontier from the Dead Sea to the Golan Heights with advanced mine clearance, multi-layered defenses, and autonomous technologies.

4M Defense deploys AI-powered robotics, ground autonomous systems, aerial drones, and subsurface sensors to map and clear unexploded ordnance far faster and safer than manual teams. CEO Eric Brock called the award proof that the operating platform converts targeted investments into operational programs that expand backlog and position the company for continued growth.

Oshri Lugassy, Co-CEO of Ondas Autonomous Systems, highlighted the competitive tender win as validation of executing complex, large-scale clearance in challenging environments. No matter how you slice it, this demining effort equals roughly 31% of Ondas’ entire 2025 revenue while serving as an entry point for hundreds of millions more in related border security work.

What the Moves Mean for Ondas

Granted, the Mistral deal still needs to close and integration carries typical execution risks common to any acquisition. International programs like the Israel initiative can also face milestone timing shifts. That said, the combination creates an end-to-end offering: Ondas’ autonomous hardware, Mistral’s prime contracting muscle, and Palantir’s Artificial Intelligence Platform for mission planning, production, and edge operations. The partnership targets persistent intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance across stratosphere, air, and ground domains -- exactly the multi-domain systems DoD budgets now prioritize.

For context, Ondas’ first-quarter 2026 revenue target sits at $38 million to $40 million, an 820% year-over-year jump, driven by the growing backlog and these new assets. While larger drone peers like AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV) deliver steadier but slower expansion from established primes, Ondas’ trajectory shows the acceleration possible when a smaller player gains prime status, AI integration, and proven international execution at once. In any case, the company now competes for larger, longer-term program-of-record opportunities instead of one-off pilots.

Key Takeaway

In short, these moves are not abstract strategy -- they are revenue catalysts already hitting the books or launching in weeks. As a small-cap stock, expect volatility, but the data shows Ondas has shifted from speculative autonomous play to a contract-backed growth story with real scale potential. Smart investors will be watching Ondas' backlog updates on the next earnings call, as that’s where the next upside will show up first.

title: Is Ondas Inc. (ONDS) A Good Stock To Buy Now?
content:
Is ONDS a good stock to buy? We came across a bullish thesis on Ondas Inc. on r/AssymetricAlpha by mtc-creative. In this article, we will summarize the bulls’ thesis on ONDS. Ondas Inc.'s share was trading at $8.80 as of March 27th.Is ONDS a good stock to buy?

Ondas Holdings (ONDS) is an early-stage, high-risk investment focused on autonomous drone systems and private industrial wireless networks, positioning itself as a venture-style opportunity rather than a core holding. The company operates through two main platforms: Ondas Autonomous Systems (OAS), which offers “drone-in-a-box” solutions for security, industrial, and government applications, and Ondas Networks, which provides mission-critical wireless connectivity for sectors like rail and utilities.

Read More: 15 AI Stocks That Are Quietly Making Investors Rich

Read More:Undervalued AI Stock Poised For Massive Gains: 10000% Upside Potential

The investment thesis centers on scaling deployments in high-value use cases such as public safety and infrastructure monitoring, where reliability and autonomy command premium pricing, though sales cycles remain long and project-driven.

The company has demonstrated rapid revenue growth, with 2025 revenue expected to reach approximately $48–50 million, supported by strong quarterly momentum, including a significant ramp into Q4. However, profitability remains distant, as operating expenses continue to outpace gross profit despite improving margins.

Management has issued aggressive forward guidance, targeting $170–180 million in revenue by 2026, implying steep growth that introduces considerable execution risk. At the same time, Ondas has significantly strengthened its balance sheet through a large equity-linked capital raise, including a $1 billion offering and potential additional proceeds from warrant exercises, providing substantial liquidity for expansion but creating meaningful dilution risk for shareholders.

Strategically, the company is gaining traction through initiatives like its “Core+” program and recent defense contracts in the Asia-Pacific region, which validate its positioning in mission-critical applications.

However, Ondas operates in highly competitive markets against larger, better-capitalized players, and its business model remains unproven at scale. While the strong cash position reduces near-term financial risk, long-term value creation will depend heavily on execution, contract conversion, and disciplined capital allocation, making ONDS a speculative, high-volatility investment with asymmetric upside.

Story Continues

Previously, we covered a bullish thesis on Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) by Kroker Equity Research in May 2025, which highlighted the company’s transformation into a software-driven, AI and cloud infrastructure platform, supported by recurring revenue growth and the Splunk acquisition. CSCO’s stock price has appreciated by approximately 25.03% since our coverage. mtc-creative shares a similar view but emphasizes on Ondas Holdings’ speculative growth and higher execution risks.

Ondas Inc. is not on our list of the 40 Most Popular Stocks Among Hedge Funds. As per our database, 29 hedge fund portfolios held ONDS at the end of the fourth quarter which was 20 in the previous quarter. While we acknowledge the risk and potential of ONDS as an investment, our conviction lies in the belief that some AI stocks hold greater promise for delivering higher returns and doing so within a shorter time frame. If you are looking for an AI stock that is more promising than ONDS and that has 10,000% upside potential, check out our report about this cheapest AI stock.

Disclosure: None.

title: Ondas Expands Into Stratospheric Intelligence With Palantir Defense Platform Bet
content:
Track your investments for FREE with Simply Wall St, the portfolio command center trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Ondas has completed its acquisition of World View Enterprises, expanding into stratospheric intelligence and surveillance capabilities. The company is launching a unified, AI enabled multi domain defense platform in partnership with Palantir Technologies. The transaction and new platform aim to integrate persistent, layered intelligence across air and ground domains for defense and security operations.

Ondas, trading on NasdaqCM:ONDS, is moving beyond its core wireless and automation roots with this World View deal and new defense platform. The stock last closed at $9.6, with a very large 1 year return and an 899.8% 3 year return, alongside a 9.0% 5 year gain. This highlights how much attention the story around the company has attracted. In the short term, the shares show mixed moves, up 1.7% over the past week and seeing a 4.2% decline over the past month.

For investors, the key question now is how effectively Ondas can integrate World View and turn this AI enabled, multi domain defense platform with Palantir into durable revenue streams. The company has already seen a very large 1 year return, so fresh developments such as execution on new contracts, customer adoption and capital needs are likely to be closely watched.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Ondas by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Ondas.NasdaqCM:ONDS Earnings & Revenue Growth as at Apr 2026

3 things going right for Ondas that this headline doesn't cover.

The World View acquisition and expanded partnership with Palantir push Ondas deeper into defense and security, tying together its drones, counter-drone systems and now near space balloons on a single, AI enabled platform. For a company that reported US$50.73 million of sales in 2025 and a net loss of US$132.02 million, investors may see this as an attempt to build higher value, multi domain offerings that could support larger, contract based revenue over time. At the same time, the move comes after a period of aggressive growth targets and widened losses, which can increase execution pressure.

How This Fits Into The Ondas Narrative

The deal lines up with the existing focus on autonomous systems and defense contracts, reinforcing the idea that Ondas wants to be a key provider of mission critical, AI enabled networks and aerial platforms. Bringing together five recent acquisitions, World View and Palantir on one architecture raises integration complexity, which is a concern already highlighted in the narrative around operating expenses and adjusted EBITDA losses. The extension into stratospheric intelligence is a fresh angle that may not be fully reflected in earlier expectations that centered more on drones, counter drone demand and private wireless networks.

Story Continues

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Ondas to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

⚠️ Ondas remains loss making, with a 2025 net loss of US$132.02 million, so heavier investment in acquisitions and product development could keep cash burn elevated. ⚠️ Frequent deals and a US$36.66 million shelf registration for over 4.0 million shares point to ongoing funding needs and the possibility of further shareholder dilution. 🎁 Integrating World View with Palantir’s software could allow Ondas to offer a broader surveillance and intelligence stack than many drone focused peers such as AeroVironment or Unusual Machines. 🎁 Management targets for very large 2026 sales and contributions from five announced acquisitions suggest a larger addressable opportunity across defense and critical infrastructure customers.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how quickly Ondas converts this platform story into contracts, especially in defense and security markets where buyers often seek long term suppliers. Progress updates on World View integration, details of any multi year agreements that use the joint Ondas, World View and Palantir system, and evidence that operating expenses are not running too far ahead of sales will be important markers. Investors may also want to track any use of the new shelf registration and how future capital raising affects per share metrics.

title: Ondas Inc (ONDS) Climbs 8.97% as Firm Ventures Into ‘Near-Space’ Industry
content:
Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) is one of the

10 Stocks With Easy 8-20% Gains.

Ondas saw its share prices jump by 8.97 percent on Thursday to finish at $9.60 apiece as investors cheered its foray into the “near-space” industry with its successful acquisition of a company engaged in flying stratospheric balloons into higher altitudes.

In a statement on the same day, Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) said that it completed the purchase of World View Enterprises Inc.—a pioneer in stratospheric intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) and high-altitude remote sensing using high-altitude balloons—extending its operational architecture beyond drones and robotics.

Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) said that the acquisition was aimed at creating a unified, multi-domain platform designed to meet the evolving requirements of modern defense and security operations through flying long-endurance, high-altitude sensing capable of persisting over areas of interest over a long period.Ondas Inc (ONDS) Climbs 8.97% as Firm Ventures Into 'Near-Space' Industry

“Demand for persistent, layered ISR and integrated mission systems is accelerating, driven by active defense modernization programs and increasing operational demand for persistent coverage. We believe Defense and homeland security customers are shifting away from siloed platforms toward software-defined, interoperable architectures capable of delivering continuous intelligence and real-time operational coordination,” Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) said.

“With the addition of World View, Ondas is uniquely positioned to address this need with a unified platform that connects detection, collection, data fusion, and response across distributed environments,” it noted.

While we acknowledge the potential of ONDS as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk.
